titlestmt.titleproper: Charles Lewis Bowman photographs and drawings3807
titlestmt.author: Compiled by Nancy Dean

publicationstmt.publisher: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections

profiledesc.creation: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on 2023-01-18 23:19:13 UTC.

change.item: This resource was modified by the ArchivesSpace Preprocessor developed by the Harvard Library (https://github.com/harvard-library/archivesspace-preprocessor)

repository.corpname: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections

did.unittitle: Charles Lewis Bowman photographs and drawings
did.abstract: Photographs and architectural drawings of houses designed or altered by Charles Lewis Bowman.

defitem.label: Contact Information:
defitem.item: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections 2B Carl A. Kroch Library Cornell University Ithaca, NY 14853 (607) 255-3530 Fax: (607) 255-9524 rareref@cornell.edu http://rmc.library.cornell.edu

bioghist.head: BIOGRAPHICAL NOTE
bioghist.p: Charles Lewis Bowman was an architect who designed many homes in Bronxville, New York and elsewhere.

scopecontent.head: COLLECTION DESCRIPTION
scopecontent.p: Photographs and architectural drawings of houses designed or altered by Charles Lewis Bowman.

prefercite.head: Cite As:
prefercite.p: Charles Lewis Bowman photographs and drawings, #3807. Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections, Cornell University Library.

did.unittitle: Series I. Photographs

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photographs of houses designed or altered by Lewis Bowman; the photographs are mainly the work of John Wallace Gillies ca. 1885-1927.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Photographs mistakenly labeled as 16 Eastway. Error identified by current property owners (2020-10-16) of property: "The four pictures that were sent and are listed as 16 Eastway are actually pictures of 15 Eastway. Charles Lewis Bowman was the architect for both houses. We currently own and live in 16 Eastway." The physical items are labeled as 16 Eastway so it's quite possible that the original photographer got confused and labeled the pictures incorrectly.
